s: Where Marks & Spencer began as a penny bazaar
w: In 1884, Michael Marks pinned up a sign at his trestle stall here: 'Don't ask the price -- it's a penny.' That penny bazaar grew into M&S. The 1904 hall, Grade I listed, is the largest covered market in Europe -- iron columns, Art Nouveau glass, 800 stalls.
_facts: Michael Marks opened a penny bazaar stall at Kirkgate Market in 1884
The 1904 hall features Flemish/Art Nouveau styling with ornamental iron and glass roof
Grade I listed building, largest covered market in Europe
A devastating fire in December 1975 destroyed most of the rear halls
M&S returned in 2012 with a commemorative stall near the centenary clock

s: Leeds's oldest arcade, with a clock that jousts on the hour
w: Opened in 1878, the oldest of Leeds's Victorian shopping arcades. At the Briggate end, a Gothic clock by Potts of Leeds features figures from Ivanhoe -- Robin Hood, Friar Tuck, Richard Coeur de Lion, Gurth -- who strike the hours. Grade II* listed.
_facts: Opened 1878, oldest of Leeds's Victorian shopping arcades
Gothic Revival interior with a hammer-beam roof
Clock by Potts of Leeds features animated Ivanhoe characters striking the hours
Grade II* listed building
One of four surviving Victorian arcades in Leeds city centre

s: Frank Matcham's arcades under Britain's largest stained-glass roof
w: Theatre architect Frank Matcham designed these baroque terracotta arcades in 1898-1904. In 1990, Brian Clarke glazed the street between them with 749 sq metres of mouth-blown antique glass -- the largest stained-glass work in Britain. Harvey Nichols arrived in 1996 as their first store outside London.
_facts: County and Cross Arcades designed by Frank Matcham, completed 1904
Brian Clarke's stained-glass canopy (1988-1990) covers 125 metres of Queen Victoria Street
749 sq metres of mouth-blown antique and opaque glass, largest stained-glass work in Britain
Harvey Nichols opened here in 1996 as their first UK store outside London
Grade II* listed; restored 1989-1996 after falling into disrepair

s: Brodrick's elliptical dome, modelled on a Paris grain market
w: Cuthbert Brodrick -- also responsible for the Town Hall -- completed this oval-plan exchange in 1864, basing the dome on the Halle aux Bles in Paris. Diamond-textured stone, round-headed windows and a lantern roof flood the trading floor with light. Grade I listed, it now hosts independent shops and food stalls.
_facts: Designed by Cuthbert Brodrick, completed 1864
Elliptical plan; dome modelled on the Halle aux Bles in Paris (1811)
Diamond-textured Italianate stonework, two tiers of round-headed windows
Grade I listed building since 1951
One of only three remaining corn exchanges still functioning as a trading centre in Britain

s: The world's only surviving gaslit cinema
w: Opened in November 1914, this is the only cinema in the world still lit by gas -- 'modesty lights' that prevent total darkness. Burmantofts terracotta on the facade; gilded cherubs still on the screen wall. The Leeds International Film Festival started here in 1987. Nearly closed 1989; saved by the council.
_facts: Opened 7 November 1914, designed by Thomas Winn & Sons
Only remaining gaslit cinema in the world
Facade features Dutch gable with Ionic columns made from Burmantofts Marmo
Leeds International Film Festival began here in 1987
Underwent 3.6 million pound renovation 2021-2023, reopening 30 June 2023

s: The world's oldest continuously working railway, running since 1758
w: Parliament authorised this colliery line in 1758 -- the oldest railway by Act of Parliament. In 1812 the Salamanca, designed by Leeds engineer Matthew Murray, hauled coal here: the first commercially successful steam locomotive in history. The line still runs, kept by volunteers.
_facts: Founded 1758 as a colliery line, oldest railway authorised by Act of Parliament
In 1812 became first commercial railway to successfully use steam locomotives
The Salamanca, designed by Matthew Murray, used John Blenkinsop's rack-and-pinion system
First professional train driver was James Hewitt, a former pit worker
Reopened as heritage railway by Leeds University volunteers in 1960

s: Three Italian campaniles built to make steel pins for the wool trade
w: Three towers modelled on Italian campaniles mark what was a textile-pin factory beside the Leeds and Liverpool Canal. The 1866 Verona Tower copies the Torre dei Lamberti; the 1899 Giotto Tower copies Giotto's Campanile, complete with ventilation ducts for steel dust. Factory closed 1981.
_facts: Factory established by T. R. Harding to make steel pins for textile carding and combing
Verona Tower (1866) styled after Torre dei Lamberti in Verona
Giotto Tower (1899) based on Giotto's Campanile in Florence, with advanced dust-extraction ventilation
Tuscan Tower (1919) in Tuscan tower-house style
Factory closed 1981 after 117 years; towers are Grade II* and Grade II listed

s: A 29-year-old's competition win that drew 400,000 to see the Queen
w: Cuthbert Brodrick was 29 when he won the competition for this Neoclassical colossus. Queen Victoria opened it in September 1858 to a crowd of 400,000 -- 32,000 schoolchildren alone on Woodhouse Moor. The 225-foot clock tower was an afterthought; it stood as the tallest structure in Leeds for 108 years.
_facts: Designed by Cuthbert Brodrick, who won the competition aged 29
Opened 7 September 1858 by Queen Victoria and Prince Albert
Crowds estimated at 400,000-600,000; 32,000 schoolchildren on Woodhouse Moor
225-foot clock tower was not in the original design, added in 1856
Tallest building in Leeds for 108 years (1858-1966); Grade I listed

s: Leeds's only surviving pre-Civil War church, saved from the wrecking ball
w: Wool merchant John Harrison built this in 1634, and the Jacobean interior -- carved screen, gilded roof pendants, brass chandeliers -- is what Pevsner called 'the only church at Leeds of more than local interest.' Victorian planners tried to demolish it; Scott and Norman Shaw fought to save it.
_facts: Built 1631-1634, funded by wool merchant John Harrison
Described by Pevsner as 'the only church at Leeds of more than local interest'
Elaborate Jacobean-style interior with carved screen, gilded pendants, brass chandeliers
Rescued from planned demolition by campaigns led by Norman Shaw and George Gilbert Scott
Grade I listed; made redundant 1975, now maintained by Churches Conservation Trust

s: A complete Cistercian ruin that inspired Turner and Girtin
w: Twelve monks from Fountains Abbey founded this Cistercian house around 1152 on land from Henry de Lacy, completing it in barely thirty years -- an unusually intact plan. Surrendered to Henry VIII in 1539, it became a Romantic pilgrimage: Turner, Girtin and Cotman all came to paint it.
_facts: Founded c.1152 by Cistercian monks from Fountains Abbey on land donated by Henry de Lacy
Built in Millstone Grit quarried from Bramley Fall; most buildings completed 1152-1182
Surrendered to Henry VIII's commissioners 22 November 1539
Attracted Romantic artists: Turner, Girtin, Cotman all painted it
Leeds Corporation acquired it in 1895; Grade I listed and scheduled ancient monument

s: The mummy that survived the Blitz and was made to speak in 2020
w: Nesyamun, a Karnak temple scribe dead since about 1100 BC, arrived in Leeds in 1824 and survived the 1941 Blitz that destroyed two other mummies. In 2020, researchers CT-scanned his vocal tract and 3D-printed it to generate his voice -- a single sustained vowel. Inside Brodrick's Mechanics' Institute.
_facts: Nesyamun purchased by Leeds banker John Blayds from antiquities dealer William Bullock in 1824
Was the only mummy undamaged in the Leeds Blitz bombing of 15 March 1941
In 2020 researchers 3D-printed his vocal tract from CT scan data to generate a simulated vowel
Building is Brodrick's Mechanics' Institute of 1865-1868
Collections include a Bengal tiger preserved with arsenical soap and on display for over 150 years

s: Britain's largest housing estate, inspired by Vienna, demolished in shame
w: From 1938 to 1978 this hillside held the UK's largest housing complex -- 938 flats modelled on Vienna's Karl-Marx-Hof, with a pool and a pneumatic Garchey refuse system. Steel panels rusted, social problems mounted, and by 1978 the whole thing was rubble. Leeds Playhouse stands here now.
_facts: Quarry Hill Flats built 1938, designed by R. A. H. Livett
Largest social housing complex in the UK at the time, with 938 flats
Design inspired by Karl-Marx-Hof in Vienna and La Cite de la Muette in Paris
Featured innovative Garchey pneumatic refuse disposal system and communal swimming pool
Demolished by 1978 due to structural deterioration and social problems; site now holds Leeds Playhouse

s: Pablo Fanque -- the Beatles' circus man -- is buried here, headstones gone
w: Leeds General Cemetery from 1835 to 1969, 93,569 interments -- the headstones cleared in 1968 without exhumation, the dead still underfoot beneath the university grass. Pablo Fanque, Britain's first Black circus owner, is here; his 1843 playbill became John Lennon's 'Being for the Benefit of Mr. Kite!'
_facts: Leeds General Cemetery opened 1835, closed 1969 with 93,569 total interments
Headstones and memorials removed 1968 without exhumation; human remains still beneath
Pablo Fanque (1810-1871), first recorded Black circus owner in Britain, buried here
John Lennon found an 1843 Fanque circus playbill and used it to write 'Being for the Benefit of Mr. Kite!' for Sgt. Pepper's
Notable burials include artist Atkinson Grimshaw and abolitionist Wilson Armistead

s: Eighteen million bricks tunnel the River Aire under Leeds station
w: When Leeds station was extended in 1866-1869, engineers used more than 18 million bricks to vault the River Aire, the canal terminus and Neville Street in a single vast undercroft. Permanently damp, perpetually rumbling -- this is where the Leeds and Liverpool Canal ends and the city begins.
_facts: Constructed 1866-1869 as part of Leeds Central Station extension
Over 18 million bricks used in construction
Arches span the River Aire, Neville Street and Swinegate
Adjacent to the terminus of the Leeds and Liverpool Canal
Artist Hans Peter Kuhn installed 'Light Neville Street' illuminated art project in 2009, decommissioned 2022

s: Napoleonic veterans dug the lake; a steamboat now rusts on the bottom
w: Waterloo Lake was dug around 1813-1815 by soldiers returning from the Napoleonic Wars, named to honour them. Thirty-three acres, sixty feet deep. A pleasure steamboat called the Maid of Athens once ran excursions here and was sunk in the lake when she retired. She is still on the bottom.
_facts: Waterloo Lake dug by unemployed Napoleonic War veterans circa 1813-1815
Named after the Battle of Waterloo to commemorate those veterans
33 acres, averages 60 feet deep
Pleasure steamboat Maid of Athens operated on the lake and was sunk there at end of service
Prince Arthur officially reopened the park in 1872 before 100,000 people; first public electric tram with overhead power in Britain ran near here in 1891

s: One of Britain's oldest continuously operating cinemas, open since 1912
w: Built as a garage for Castle Grove mansion in 1905, newsreel cameraman Owen Brooks opened it as a cinema in July 1912 -- sixpence for the stalls. The national anthem still plays at the end of every evening. Ice cream is still served inside. Nearly closed in 2005; saved by a last-minute buyout.
_facts: Originally built 1905 as a garage for Castle Grove mansion
Opened as Headingley Picture House on 29 July 1912 by newsreel cameraman Owen Brooks
National anthem played at end of each evening; ice cream served in auditorium
Nearly closed in 2005 but saved by Northern Morris Group last-minute buyout
Celebrated 100th anniversary with blue plaque in 2012; capacity 466 seats

s: Home of the oldest Caribbean carnival in Europe, born in 1967
w: In 1967, Arthur France -- a Leeds student from Nevis -- organised the first Caribbean outdoor carnival in Europe in this Chapeltown park. Around 1,000 people came. By its 40th anniversary in 2007, the crowd was 100,000. The carnival still runs every August bank holiday, the oldest of its kind on the continent.
_facts: Leeds West Indian Carnival founded 1967 by Arthur France, a student from Nevis
First Caribbean-style outdoor carnival organised by people of Caribbean origin in Europe
First event had approximately 1,000 attendees and a procession to Leeds Town Hall
40th anniversary in 2007 attracted 100,000 attendees, coinciding with the bicentenary of abolition
Arthur France received an MBE in 1997 for his community work; Ian Charles MBE in 2007
